problem.title: Binary Search Tree Iterator
problem.description: Implement the BSTIterator class that represents an iterator over the in-order traversal of a binary search tree (BST).
assumptions: next() returns the next smallest number.
hasNext() returns true if more elements exist.
next() and hasNext() run in O(1) average time with O(h) space where h = tree height.

example.input:
root = [7,3,15,null,null,9,20]
next() → 3, next() → 7, next() → 9, hasNext() → true, next() → 15
example.explanation: In-order traversal of BST yields sorted order: 3,7,9,15,20.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Stack [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: Simulate inorder traversal lazily using an explicit stack: initialise by pushing all left-spine nodes, then each call to next() pops the top node, pushes the right child's left spine, and returns the value. Because each node is pushed and popped exactly once across all next() calls, the amortized time per call is O(1) while using only O(h) space (h = tree height). The key insight is that the stack always holds the 'pending' left-spine of the current subtree.
